Front: How is building product evals 'the scientific method in disguise'? Map the steps.
Back: Observation (we see failures, e.g. hallucinations) -> hypothesis (a specific change, e.g. constrain to governmental sources, will reduce them) -> experiment (run on a FIXED eval set, measure a metric, compare to baseline) -> analysis (inspect remaining failures, find the pattern, form the next hypothesis) -> repeat. The control is holding the dataset constant.

Front: In eval work, what plays the role of the 'control' and why does it matter?
Back: A fixed eval set (same contexts/questions/expected answers) measured before and after a change. Without holding the dataset constant you cannot attribute an improvement to your change versus noise.

Front: Where does the EDD-vs-TDD analogy break down?
Back: A TDD test is a binary, deterministic assertion (red/green). An eval result is an aggregate metric over a distribution (e.g. hallucination rate) compared to a threshold and baseline. 'Success' becomes a judgment call about whether the number moved beyond noise and whether tradeoffs (some hallucination for more speed/breadth) are acceptable. Evals are also noisy/non-deterministic, so one run is not gospel.

r//3 = box row (rows 0-2->0, 3-5->1, 6-8->2). c//3 = box column (same). Multiplying the box row by 3 flattens the 2D box coordinate (box_row, box_col) into a 1D index, because there are 3 boxes per row. This is row-major flattening; box_idx spans 0-8 with no collisions.

Front: Valid Sudoku: what is the optimal approach and complexity?
Back: Single pass over all 81 cells. Keep three lists of 9 sets: rows, cols, boxes. For each filled cell, if its value is already in the matching row/col/box set, return False; else add it to all three. O(1) for the fixed 9x9 board (O(n^2) for an n x n generalization), O(1) extra space.

Front: Docker: runc exits after creating the container but the shim stays. What two jobs does the shim do, and what does that enable?
Back: (1) Manages the container's I/O streams (STDIN/STDOUT/STDERR). (2) Reports the container's exit status back up. Because the shim, not dockerd, parents the container and holds the streams, you get daemonless containers: you can stop/restart/upgrade the Docker daemon without killing running containers (zero-downtime engine upgrades).
